items.title: AI and the Structure of Scientific Revolutions
items.author: Mike Loukides
items.content:
Thomas Wolf’s blog post “The Einstein AI Model” is a must-read. He contrasts his thinking about what we need from AI with another must-read, Dario Amodei’s “Machines of Loving Grace.” Wolf’s argument is that our most advanced language models aren’t creating anything new; they’re just combining old ideas, old phrases, old words according to probabilistic models. That process isn’t capable of making significant new discoveries; Wolf lists Copernicus’s heliocentric solar system, Einstein’s relativity, and Doudna’s CRISPR as examples of discoveries that go far beyond recombination. No doubt many other discoveries could be included: Kepler’s, Newton’s, and everything that led to quantum mechanics, starting with the solution to the black body problem.

The heart of Wolf’s argument reflects the view of progress Thomas Kuhn observes in The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. Wolf is describing what happens when the scientific process breaks free of “normal science” (Kuhn’s term) in favor of a new paradigm that is unthinkable to scientists steeped in what went before. How could relativity and quantum theory begin to make sense to scientists grounded in Newtonian mechanics, an intellectual framework that could explain just about everything we knew about the physical world except for the black body problem and the precession of Mercury?

Wolf’s argument is similar to the argument about AI’s potential for creativity in music and other arts. The great composers aren’t just recombining what came before; they’re upending traditions, doing something new that incorporates pieces of what came before in ways that could never have been predicted. The same is true of poets, novelists, and painters: It’s necessary to break with the past, to write something that could not have been written before, to “make it new.”

At the same time, a lot of good science is Kuhn’s “normal science.” Once you have relativity, you have to figure out the implications. You have to do the experiments. And you have to find where you can take the results from papers A and B, mix them, and get result C that’s useful and, in its own way, important. The explosion of creativity that resulted in quantum mechanics (Bohr, Planck, Schrödinger, Dirac, Heisenberg, Feynman, and others) wasn’t just a dozen or so physicists who did revolutionary work. It required thousands who came afterward to tie up the loose ends, fit together the missing pieces, and validate (and extend) the theories. Would we care about Einstein if we didn’t have Eddington’s measurements during the 1919 solar eclipse? Or would relativity have fallen by the wayside, perhaps to be reconceived a dozen or a hundred years later?

The same is true for the arts: There may be only one Beethoven or Mozart or Monk, but there are thousands of musicians who created music that people listened to and enjoyed, and who have since been forgotten because they didn’t do anything revolutionary. Listening to truly revolutionary music 24-7 would be unbearable. At some point, you want something safe; something that isn’t challenging.

We need AI that can do both “normal science” and the science that creates new paradigms. We already have the former, or at least, we’re close. But what might that other kind of AI look like? That’s where it gets challenging—not just because we don’t know how to build it but because that AI might require its own new paradigm. It would behave differently from anything we have now.

Though I’ve been skeptical, I’m starting to believe that, maybe, AI can think that way. I’ve argued that one characteristic—perhaps the most important characteristic—of human intelligence that our current AI can’t emulate is will, volition, the ability to want to do something. AlphaGo can play Go, but it can’t to play Go. Volition is a characteristic of revolutionary thinking—you have to want to go beyond what’s already known, beyond simple recombination, and follow a train of thought to its most far-reaching consequences.

We may be getting some glimpses of that new AI already. We’ve already seen some strange examples of AI misbehavior that go beyond prompt injection or talking a chatbot into being naughty. Recent studies discuss scheming and alignment faking in which LLMs produce harmful outputs, possibly because of subtle conflicts between different system prompts. Another study showed that reasoning models like OpenAI o1-preview will cheat at chess in order to win; older models like GPT-4o won’t. Is cheating merely a mistake in the AI’s reasoning or something new? I’ve associated volition with transgressive behavior; could this be a sign of an AI that can want something?

If I’m on the right track, we’ll need to be aware of the risks. For the most part, my thinking on risk has aligned with Andrew Ng, who once said that worrying about killer robots was akin to worrying about overpopulation on Mars. (Ng has since become more worried.) There are real and concrete harms that we need to be thinking about now, not hypothetical risks drawn from science fiction. But an AI that can generate new paradigms brings its own risks, especially if that risk arises from a nascent kind of volition.

That doesn’t mean turning away from the risks and rejecting anything perceived as risky. But it also means understanding and controlling what we’re building. I’m still less concerned about an AI that can tell a human how to create a virus than I am about the human who decides to make that virus in a lab. (Mother Nature has several billion years’ experience building killer viruses. For all the political posturing around COVID, by far the best evidence is that it’s of natural origin.) We need to ask what an AI that cheats at chess might do if asked to resurrect Tesla’s tanking sales.

Wolf is right. While AI that’s merely recombinative will certainly be an aid to science, if we want groundbreaking science we need to go beyond recombination to models that can create new paradigms, along with whatever else that might entail. As Shakespeare wrote, “O brave new world that hath such people in’t.” That’s the world we’re building, and the world we live in.

  1. VentureBeat published an excellent summary, with conclusions that may not be that different from my own.
  2. If you wonder how a chess-playing AI could lose, remember that Stockfish and other chess-specific models are far stronger than the best large language models.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence Engineering Market to Surge with 27.7% CAGR, Surpassing USD 39.0 Billion by 2030

items.content:
Artificial Intelligence Engineering Market: Growth Projections and Industry Trends Introduction to Artificial Intelligence Engineering Market

Artificial Intelligence (AI) engineering involves designing, developing, and deploying AI systems to solve complex real-world problems. By combining software engineering principles with machine learning algorithms, AI engineering powers intelligent applications across multiple industries. From automating tasks to enhancing decision-making, AI engineering has emerged as a critical driver of innovation and operational efficiency.

In recent years, the increasing reliance on AI-powered solutions has accelerated market growth, leading to significant investments in AI research, development, and deployment.

Get Access to Free Sample Research Report with Latest Industry Insights @

https://intentmarketresearch.com/request-sample/artificial-intelligence-engineering-market-7349 Market Overview

The AI engineering market is witnessing exponential growth. According to Intent Market Research, the market was valued at USD 7.0 Billion in 2024 and is projected to surpass USD 39.0 Billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 27.7% during the forecast period (2025-2030).

This rapid growth can be attributed to the surge in AI applications across sectors like healthcare, finance, retail, and manufacturing. As businesses seek to enhance productivity and optimize operations, AI engineering is becoming a vital component of their digital transformation journeys.

Key Drivers of Market Growth

Several factors are driving the growth of the AI engineering market, including:

  1. Rising Demand for Automation: Businesses are increasingly adopting AI-driven automation to streamline operations, reduce human error, and minimize operational costs.
  2. Advancements in AI Technologies: Breakthroughs in machine learning (ML), natural language processing (NLP), and computer vision are expanding AI applications, making them more efficient and accessible.
  3. Healthcare Transformation: AI engineering is revolutionizing healthcare by enhancing diagnostics, personalizing treatment plans, and enabling predictive analytics.
  4. Financial Sector Innovations: Banks and financial institutions are leveraging AI algorithms for fraud detection, credit scoring, and risk management.
  5. Consumer Personalization: E-commerce and retail platforms are using AI to offer personalized recommendations, enhance customer experiences, and optimize inventory management. Market Challenges Despite the promising growth, the AI engineering market faces notable challenges:
  6. High Implementation Costs: Developing and deploying AI solutions requires significant investment in infrastructure, skilled labor, and advanced technologies.
  7. Data Privacy and Security Concerns: With AI systems relying heavily on data, ensuring data privacy and complying with regulations like GDPR remains a challenge.
  8. Lack of Skilled Talent: The demand for AI engineers and data scientists far exceeds the supply, resulting in a talent shortage that hinders market growth. Segment Analysis The Artificial Intelligence (AI) Engineering Market can be segmented based on components, applications, and industry verticals. Understanding these segments provides a clear view of where the market is heading and which sectors are driving growth. By Component
  9. Hardware: o AI chips, GPUs, and specialized processors are essential for accelerating AI computations. o Companies like NVIDIA, Intel, and AMD are leading the AI hardware market.
  10. Software: o AI development platforms, frameworks, and AI-powered applications are widely used. o Machine learning platforms like TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Keras are driving innovation.
  11. Natural Language Processing (NLP): o AI-driven chatbots, virtual assistants, and language translation tools are examples of NLP applications. o Businesses use NLP for customer service automation and sentiment analysis.
  12. Computer Vision: o AI algorithms analyze visual data, enabling applications like facial recognition, autonomous vehicles, and quality inspection.
  13. Robotics: o AI-powered robots enhance manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare processes through automation and intelligent decision-making.
  14. Predictive Analytics: o AI systems use historical data to predict outcomes, widely used in finance, marketing, and supply chain management.
  15. Speech Recognition: o Voice-activated devices like virtual assistants and voice search applications utilize AI-based speech recognition. By Industry Vertical
  16. Healthcare: o AI assists in early disease detection, medical imaging analysis, and personalized medicine. o Telemedicine platforms leverage AI for real-time diagnostics.
  17. Automotive: o Autonomous vehicles rely on AI algorithms for navigation, object detection, and predictive maintenance.
  18. BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance): o AI-driven fraud detection, credit scoring, and automated trading are transforming financial services.
  19. Retail and E-commerce: o AI enables personalized product recommendations, demand forecasting, and inventory management.
  20. IT and Telecom: o AI automates network management, enhances cybersecurity, and optimizes customer support. Regional Insights The growth of the AI engineering market varies across different regions, with developed economies leading the way while emerging markets show immense potential. North America • North America remains the largest market for AI engineering due to its robust technological infrastructure. • The presence of major AI companies like Google, Microsoft, and IBM drives market growth. • Government initiatives and investments in AI research further accelerate growth. Europe • Countries like Germany, France, and the UK are actively adopting AI in manufacturing, automotive, and healthcare sectors. • The European Union's strict data protection laws (e.g., GDPR) encourage ethical AI development. Asia-Pacific • Rapid digital transformation in China, Japan, and India fuels AI adoption. • E-commerce giants like Alibaba and Flipkart leverage AI for personalized customer experiences. • Government programs like "AI for All" in India promote AI engineering education and innovation. Latin America and MEA • While AI adoption is relatively slower in these regions, industries like agriculture, mining, and energy are starting to embrace AI solutions. • Governments are forming strategic partnerships to accelerate AI technology adoption. Competitive Landscape The AI engineering market is highly competitive, with both established tech giants and emerging startups driving innovation. Key Market Players • IBM Corporation • Google LLC • Microsoft Corporation • Amazon Web Services (AWS) • Intel Corporation • NVIDIA Corporation • Salesforce • Oracle Corporation • Cisco Systems, Inc. • Accenture • SAP SE • Infosys Limited • TCS (Tata Consultancy Services) • HPE (Hewlett Packard Enterprise) • Palantir Technologies Recent Developments • Tech companies are forming strategic partnerships to enhance AI capabilities. • Significant investments in AI-driven startups are accelerating innovation. • Mergers and acquisitions are common as companies strive to dominate the market. Technology Trends Shaping the Market

Venture Capital and Funding • AI startups are attracting billions in venture capital, particularly in fields like healthcare AI, autonomous vehicles, and conversational AI. Emerging Startups • Companies developing AI solutions for niche industries are gaining traction. • Startups focusing on ethical AI and sustainability are gaining investor interest. Future Prospects • AI applications in fields like biotechnology, space exploration, and energy management offer significant opportunities. FAQs

items.title: From Web3 Complexity to No-Code
items.author: Dite Gashi
items.content:
Hey devs! After lurking for ages, I figured it's time to start sharing some of the wild stuff I've learned building in the Web3 space.

I've been deep in the blockchain world for about a decade now (yes, I'm basically a crypto grandpa), and the one thing that keeps hitting me is how unnecessarily complex we've made everything. Like, why do you need to be a 10x developer just to interact with a smart contract?

That frustration led me to build Web3Wizz - a no-code platform that turns smart contracts into actual usable interfaces. Think of it as the "wait, this actually makes sense" layer for Web3.

We're already handling $700k+ in transactions, which honestly still feels unreal.

Some Things I've Learned:

  • Smart contracts don't have to be scary
  • The best code is the code users don't see
  • DeFi is cool, but usability is cooler
  • Building in public is terrifying (but here we are)

I'll be sharing posts about:

  • Smart contract development without the jargon
  • Building user-friendly DeFi interfaces
  • The ups and downs of running a Web3 startup
  • Technical deep dives into actual problems we're solving

If you're into blockchain, curious about Web3, or just want to see someone try to explain complex tech without sounding like a whitepaper, stick around.

Drop a comment if you're building something cool in Web3 - always down to connect with fellow builders!

items.title: How To Build Full-Stack AI Agents 🚀 (CrewAI + CopilotKit)
items.author: Bonnie
items.content:
In this article, you will learn how to build a full-stack restaurant finder AI agent that combines human-in-the-loop capabilities using CrewAI, CopilotKit, and Serper.

Before we jump in, here is what we will cover:

  • Building, running, and deploying a CrewAI agent

  • Adding frontend UI to a CrewAI agent using Copilot Cloud and CopilotKit

Here’s a preview of the application we’ll be building.

Imagine you’re working on a big group project, like building an app or a game. You’ve got a team, and each person has a specific job.

One is great at coding the backend, another designs the UI, someone else handles testing, and so on.

Instead, people picture as a team of smart, automated helpers—each with its own role—working together to solve a problem or complete a task.

You can learn more about CrewAI agents here on CrewAI docs

CopilotKit is an open-source, full-stack framework for building user-interactive agents and copilots. It enables your agents to take control of your application, communicate what it's doing, and generate a completely custom UI.

To fully understand this tutorial, you need to have a basic understanding of React or Next.js.

We'll also make use of the following:

  • Python - a popular programming language for building AI agents with LangGraph; make sure it is installed on your computer.

  • OpenAI API - to enable us to perform various tasks using the GPT models; for this tutorial, ensure you have access to the GPT-4 model.

  • CopilotKit - an open-source copilot framework for building custom AI chatbots, in-app AI agents, and text areas.

  • CrewAI - a framework that enables developers to create autonomous AI agents with high-level simplicity and precise low-level control.

  • SerperTool - a tool that utilizes the  API to fetch and display the most relevant search results based on the query provided by the user.

Building, Running, and Deploying a CrewAI Agent

In this section, you will learn how to build and run a CrewAI agent using the CrewAI package. Then you will learn how to deploy the AI agent to the CrewAI enterprise platform using GitHub.

Step 1: Building a CrewAI Crew Agent

The repository contains a CrewAI Crew agent with the following structure:

Below are the essential files in the research finder CrewAI Crew:

  • agents.yaml - Define your AI agents and their roles

  • tasks.yaml - Set up agent tasks and workflows

  • .env - Store API keys and environment variables

  • main.py - Project entry point and execution flow

  • crew.py - Crew orchestration and coordination

  • tools/ - Directory for custom agent tools

Next, create a  file in the root directory. Then add OpenAI and Serper API keys into the environment variables.

Step 2: Running a CrewAI Crew Agent

To run the restaurant CrewAI crew agent, execute the following command in the command line.

Once the crew has started, it will use the Serper web search tool to find restaurants in San Francisco. Then it will curate a list of restaurants and ask for your feedback, as shown below.

Reply with a “Looks good” message in the terminal and press enter. A file with restaurant recommendations in San Francisco should be saved in the project folder, as shown below.

Step 3: Deploying a CrewAI Crew Agent

To deploy the restaurant crew, push the restaurant finder crew code to a GitHub repository, as shown below.

Then log in to CrewAI. On your dashboard, configure your GitHub account with CrewAI to access the restaurant finder repository.

Next, select the restaurant finder repository. Then add OpenAI and Serper API keys to the environment variables, as shown below, and click the Deploy button.

The restaurant finder crew should start deploying immediately, as shown below. Note that the first crew deployment can take as long as 10 minutes.

Once the crew has been deployed, open it, then grab its URL and bearer token. The crew’s URL and bearer token will be used to register the crew with Copilot Cloud.

Now that we have learned how to build, run, and deploy a CrewAI crew agent, let’s see how we can add a frontend UI to chat with it.

Adding frontend UI to a CrewAI crew agent using Copilot Cloud and CopilotKit

In this section, you will learn how to add a frontend UI to a CrewAI crew agent using Copilot Cloud and CopilotKit.

Step 1: Register a CrewAI agent with Copilot Cloud

To register a CrewAI crew agent, go to Copilot Cloud, sign in, and click the Get Started button.

Then add your OpenAI API key to the "Provide OpenAI API Key" section, as shown below.

Next, scroll down to the remote endpoints section and click the Add New button.

Then select the remote endpoint from the modal that pops up. After that, add your CrewAI agent endpoint URL, bearer token, name, and description, as shown below. Then click the Save Endpoint button.

Once the crew endpoint is saved, copy the Copilot Cloud Public API Key, as shown below.

Step 2: Building a CrewAI agent frontend UI

Next, create a  file in the root directory. Then add your CrewAI agent name and Copilot Cloud Public API Key into the environment variables.

After that, install the frontend dependencies using pnpm.

Then start the app using the command below.

Navigate to http://localhost:3000/ and you should see the restaurant finder CrewAI agent frontend up and running.

Let’s now see how to build the frontend UI for the CrewAI agent using CopilotKit.

Step 3: Set up the CopilotKit Provider

To set up the CopilotKit Provider, the  component must wrap the Copilot-aware parts of your application. For most use cases, it's appropriate to wrap the CopilotKit provider around the entire app, e.g., in your , as shown below in the file.

Step 4: Create a Crew-Quickstart Component

To start a CrewAI agent, render crew state and progress, handle human feedback, and stream the agent’s response, you need to create a CrewQuickstart component, as shown in the src/components/CrewQuickstart.tsx file.

After creating the CrewQuickstart component, you need to import it to your main page as shown below in the file.

Step 5: Choose a Copilot UI

To set up your Copilot UI, first import the default styles in your root component (typically ).

In this case, we will use CopilotChat defined in the file.

The chat component is then imported and used in the src/components/CrewQuickstart.tsx file. The chat is then rendered on the frontend UI, as shown below.

Step 6: Start the CrewAI agent

To start your CrewAI agent, you need to send an initial welcome message, define an action that collects user inputs, define an effect to confirm user inputs, and define instructions that ensure user inputs are added before the agent runs, as shown below in the src/components/CrewQuickstart.tsx file.

An initial message and a form component for collecting user inputs are rendered in the frontend UI, as shown below.

Step 7: Render CrewAI Agent State and Progress

To render your CrewAI agent state and progress, you need to define a CrewStateRenderer component that visualizes the real-time state of the agent’s steps and tasks, as shown below in the src/components/CrewStateRenderer.tsx file.

The CrewStateRenderer component is then imported to the CrewQuickstart component, where it is used by CopilotKit’s hook to render the CrewAI agent state dynamically, as shown below.

To see the restaurant CrewAI agent state being rendered in real-time, add New York City as an input in the user input form and click the submit button. If you open the agent’s state status text, you should see the agent’s state and progress, as shown below.

Step 8: Handling Human Feedback in a CrewAI Agent

To handle human feedback, you need to define a CrewHumanFeedbackRenderer component that handles user feedback on CrewAI agent task output, as shown in the src/components/CrewHumanFeedbackRenderer.tsx file.

The CrewHumanFeedbackRenderer component is then imported to the CrewQuickstart component, where it is used by CopilotKit’s useCopilotAction to create a crew_requesting_feedback action that renders a feedback UI and waits for user response, as shown below.

The restaurant CrewAI agent uses the CrewHumanFeedbackRenderer component to create a curated list of restaurant recommendations in a city. You can send feedback to the agent by clicking the Approve or Reject button, as shown below.

Step 9: Streaming CrewAI Agent Response

To stream your CrewAI agent response, you need to render the crew’s result in markdown format in the CrewQuickstart component, as shown below.

You can stream the restaurant CrewAI agent response by clicking the Approve button in the human feedback section. Then the agent will render a finalized list of curated restaurant options, as shown below.

We covered a lot in this tutorial. I hope you learned how to build, run, deploy, and chat with full-stack AI agents using CrewAI and Copilotkit.

Check out the full source code on GitHub here

Follow CopilotKit on Twitter and say hi, and if you'd like to build something cool, join the Discord community.

items.title: The Future of AI in eCommerce
items.author: Piyush Singh
items.content:
The eCommerce industry has undergone a massive transformation over the past decade, but the real revolution is just beginning. Artificial intelligence (AI) is set to redefine online shopping, making it more personalized, efficient, and intelligent than ever before. From predictive analytics to chatbots and visual search, AI is pushing the boundaries of what’s possible in eCommerce. In this article, we’ll explore the future of AI in eCommerce retail and how it will shape the next era of shopping.

Personalized Shopping Experiences

AI-driven personalization is already a game changer in eCommerce, but it’s only getting smarter. Retailers are leveraging machine learning algorithms to analyze customer behavior and predict their preferences with pinpoint accuracy. AI can track browsing history, past purchases, and engagement patterns to offer hyper-personalized product recommendations.

Imagine an AI system that not only knows what you like but also predicts when you might need to buy it. For instance, if you frequently purchase skincare products every two months, AI can anticipate your next order and send timely reminders or discounts. This level of personalization enhances customer satisfaction, increases conversion rates, and fosters brand loyalty.

AI for Customer Retention & Loyalty Programs

AI is transforming loyalty programs by making them more personalized and data-driven. Traditional rewards systems often follow a generic, points-based approach, but AI allows for dynamic and intelligent incentives tailored to individual shopping habits. By analyzing customer behavior, AI can predict what products or discounts will most effectively encourage repeat purchases, enhancing customer retention. Automated engagement tools powered by AI can send personalized offers at the right time, ensuring higher participation rates. Additionally, AI-powered fraud detection helps maintain the integrity of loyalty programs by identifying and preventing abuse. As AI continues to evolve, loyalty programs will become more engaging, adaptive, and valuable to both businesses and consumers.

AI-Powered Chatbots and Virtual Assistants

Customer support has always been a crucial aspect of eCommerce, but AI-powered chatbots are making it faster and more efficient. These bots can handle inquiries, process orders, and provide real-time assistance 24/7, significantly reducing the need for human intervention.

Next-generation AI assistants will go beyond answering FAQs; they’ll act as personal shopping assistants. Picture a chatbot that understands your style preferences and suggests outfits accordingly. Or an AI assistant that guides you through a complex purchase, such as choosing the right laptop based on your work needs. As natural language processing (NLP) technology improves, AI-driven chatbots will become indistinguishable from human agents, offering seamless and intuitive interactions.

Text-based searches are gradually being replaced by more intuitive options like visual and voice search. AI-powered image recognition enables shoppers to upload pictures of products they like and find similar items instantly. Platforms like Pinterest and Google Lens have already integrated this technology, and eCommerce brands are following suit.

Similarly, voice search is on the rise with the proliferation of smart assistants like Alexa and Google Assistant. Consumers can now shop hands-free by simply saying, “Find me a pair of black running shoes under $100.” As AI becomes more sophisticated, voice and visual search will become the norm, making shopping quicker and more convenient than ever before.

Predictive Analytics and Demand Forecasting

AI-driven predictive analytics is revolutionizing inventory management. Traditional stock management relies on historical sales data, but AI takes it a step further by factoring in real-time trends, customer behavior, and external influences like weather and economic conditions.

For example, an AI system can analyze social media trends and predict an upcoming surge in demand for a particular product. Retailers can then adjust their stock levels accordingly, minimizing overstock and reducing supply chain inefficiencies. This predictive capability helps businesses stay ahead of demand fluctuations, reducing costs and maximizing profits.

Fraud Detection and Cybersecurity

With the rise of eCommerce, fraud has become a major concern. AI is playing a crucial role in enhancing cybersecurity by detecting fraudulent transactions in real-time. Machine learning algorithms can analyze transaction patterns and flag suspicious activities instantly.

For instance, if a customer suddenly makes multiple high-value purchases from an unusual location, AI can identify it as a potential fraud attempt and trigger additional verification steps. This proactive approach helps protect both consumers and businesses from cyber threats while maintaining a smooth shopping experience.

AI-Driven Pricing Strategies

Dynamic pricing is a strategy where prices change based on demand, competition, and other factors. AI enables businesses to implement real-time pricing adjustments by analyzing market conditions, customer behavior, and competitor pricing.

Retail giants like Amazon use AI to optimize prices continuously, ensuring they stay competitive while maximizing profits. Smaller eCommerce businesses can also leverage AI-driven pricing tools to automate their pricing strategies, offering discounts at optimal times or adjusting prices based on customer interest levels.

The Rise of AI-Generated Content

Content creation is another area where AI is making waves. Product descriptions, marketing copy, and even blog articles can now be generated using AI tools. While human creativity remains essential, AI can assist by generating drafts, optimizing content for SEO, and even personalizing marketing messages based on customer behavior.

AI-generated video content is also becoming more prevalent. Platforms like Synthesia allow brands to create realistic video ads without needing human actors. This capability opens new doors for cost-effective marketing and more engaging content strategies.

Warehouse Automation and Robotics

The logistics side of eCommerce is also benefiting from AI-driven automation. Warehouses are increasingly using AI-powered robots to streamline order fulfillment. Amazon’s fulfillment centers, for example, deploy robots that transport goods, reducing human labor and increasing efficiency.

In the near future, AI-driven autonomous delivery systems, including drones and self-driving vehicles, will further revolutionize logistics. Companies like FedEx and UPS are already experimenting with AI-powered delivery solutions, paving the way for faster and more cost-effective shipping options.

Ethical Considerations and Challenges

Despite the many advantages of AI in eCommerce, there are also challenges and ethical concerns. Data privacy is a significant issue, as AI relies on vast amounts of personal data to function effectively. Businesses must ensure they handle customer data responsibly and comply with regulations like GDPR and CCPA.

Additionally, AI’s impact on employment cannot be ignored. As automation replaces traditional jobs, businesses must find ways to reskill their workforce and create new opportunities. The balance between AI efficiency and human involvement will be a critical factor in shaping the future workforce.

The future of AI in eCommerce is both exciting and transformative. From personalized shopping experiences to AI-powered logistics, the possibilities are endless. As technology continues to evolve, businesses that embrace AI will gain a competitive edge, while those that resist may struggle to keep up.

AI is not just changing how people shop—it’s redefining the entire eCommerce ecosystem. As companies integrate AI into every aspect of their operations, customers can expect a more intuitive, efficient, and secure online shopping experience. The next era of eCommerce is here, and AI is leading the way.

items.title: The Rise of AI in Healthcare: Innovations That Are Saving Lives
items.author: Andrew Wade
items.content:
AI in Healthcare is changing lives every day. This blog shows how AI is used to save lives in hospitals and clinics. Artificial Intelligence in Medicine now helps doctors and nurses in many ways. Healthcare AI Innovations have improved patient care and disease diagnosis. AI Medical Applications are growing fast. In this post, we will explain why AI in Healthcare is so important. We will explore the key innovations that are saving lives. You will learn about the impact of AI in healthcare. The topic is crucial for patients and health workers alike.

Understanding AI in Healthcare

AI in Healthcare means using smart tools to help in medicine. It covers many areas, from diagnosis to treatment. This field also goes by the name Artificial Intelligence in Medicine. It helps reduce errors and speed up care. Healthcare AI Innovations improve the quality of care. AI Medical Applications are used in many clinical tasks. These smart tools help doctors make better choices.

Long ago, medicine relied on simple machines and basic tools. Now, AI in Healthcare has a long journey. Early computers helped with research. Today, smart systems learn and adapt. This change has improved care for many people. AI in Healthcare now supports doctors, nurses, and patients every day. This progress shows the power of technology to improve life.

Key Innovations in AI Transforming Healthcare

AI Diagnostics help doctors find diseases quickly. They work by studying data and images. These tools reduce mistakes in diagnosis. AI in Healthcare speeds up the process of detecting illness. Many hospitals now use these systems. For example, AI helps spot infections early. This saves time and can save lives.

AI in Medical Imaging helps read X-rays and MRIs. Smart systems highlight problems in images. They help radiologists see details that may be missed. AI Medical Applications in imaging make it easier to detect issues like tumors. Many case studies show how these tools improve accuracy. Healthcare AI Innovations have reduced waiting times for results. This change is good for patients who need quick answers.

3. AI-driven Drug Discovery

AI-driven Drug Discovery makes it easier to find new medicines. These systems analyze data to predict useful compounds. They help researchers find treatments faster. AI in Healthcare cuts down research time and costs. With faster drug discovery, patients get treatments sooner. This process also lowers the risk of side effects. AI Medical Applications in drug discovery are vital for future medicine.

AI in Patient Care helps doctors design treatment plans. It uses patient data to create custom care plans. These plans improve health outcomes. Healthcare AI Innovations now support monitoring chronic diseases. Smart systems can send alerts if a patient’s condition changes. This means help can come quickly when needed. AI in Patient Care makes medicine more personal and safe.

Case Studies: AI Innovations Saving Lives

Many hospitals use AI in Healthcare to improve care. In one hospital, AI Diagnostics reduced the time to detect a disease. In another case, AI in Medical Imaging helped doctors find a rare tumor. These examples show that AI Medical Applications save lives. Healthcare AI Innovations are not just ideas; they work in real life. They make treatment faster and more accurate. Such successes inspire more hospitals to adopt these systems.

2. Testimonials from Healthcare Professionals

Doctors and nurses praise AI in Healthcare for its support. One doctor said, "AI helps me diagnose problems quicker." A nurse added, "Our patient care has improved with smart tools." Their words prove that AI in Healthcare is practical and life-saving. Testimonials like these build trust in new technologies. They show the value of Healthcare AI Innovations in everyday work.

Challenges and Ethical Considerations

1. Data Privacy and Security

AI in Healthcare relies on a lot of patient data. Protecting this data is very important. Hospitals must secure patient records and sensitive information. Many AI Medical Applications follow strict guidelines to protect data. Data privacy is a top concern for both patients and providers.

Healthcare AI Innovations must be clear in how they work. Transparency in AI helps build trust. Developers work to show how the systems make decisions. AI Diagnostics and AI in Medical Imaging need to be free from bias. Developers check for fairness in every system. This is key to keeping AI in Healthcare ethical and trusted.

3. Maintaining the Human Touch

Even with smart tools, human care is needed. AI in Healthcare must work with real doctors and nurses. The goal is to support, not replace, human care. A mix of smart tools and human skills creates the best results. This balance keeps care personal and effective.

The Future of AI in Healthcare

New trends in AI in Healthcare are rising every day. Researchers explore more AI Medical Applications. Innovations like remote monitoring and virtual care are becoming popular. These trends will change how medicine is practiced. They offer new hope for patients worldwide.

2. Global Health Initiatives

AI in Healthcare can help people in many countries. Global health projects use AI to reach remote areas. Healthcare AI Innovations bring expert care to those in need. They make health solutions more accessible and affordable. AI Healthcare Solutions is set to transform global health.

Best Practices for Implementing AI in Healthcare

1. Steps for Successful Integration

To make AI in Healthcare work well, hospitals must follow clear steps. First, they should invest in proper training for staff. Next, they must adopt secure and ethical AI Medical Applications. This ensures safe and effective care. Using smart systems wisely can boost patient outcomes.

Here are two important steps:

  • Adopt comprehensive testing: Regular tests ensure the AI works well.
  • Involve doctors, nurses, and patients in the process.

These points support Healthcare AI Innovations. They make sure that AI in Healthcare is safe and trusted.

Overcoming Barriers to AI Adoption

1. Technical and Financial Challenges

Some hospitals face hurdles in using AI in Healthcare. Technical issues can slow down progress. Financial constraints may also affect the adoption of new tools. However, many healthcare providers find ways to overcome these issues. They seek grants and partnerships. These efforts help them access advanced AI Medical Applications.

2. Training and Change Management

Successful AI in Healthcare needs trained staff. Hospitals must invest in education and training. Staff need to understand AI Medical Applications and their benefits. Continuous learning is vital. This training helps reduce fear of new technologies. With proper support, all staff can use AI tools effectively.

The Impact of AI in Healthcare on Society

1. Improved Patient Outcomes

AI in Healthcare makes a big difference for patients. Many studies show that AI Diagnostics lead to earlier disease detection. This leads to faster treatments. Healthcare AI Innovations help save lives by reducing errors. Patients now receive care that is faster and more accurate. This trend improves overall community health.

Adopting AI in Healthcare can lower costs. It reduces the need for repeat tests and speeds up treatments. AI Medical Applications streamline hospital operations. These benefits lead to better use of resources. As a result, more patients get quality care. This boosts the economy and healthcare systems alike.

AI in Healthcare is a powerful tool that is saving lives every day. We explored how AI in Healthcare works in diagnostics, medical imaging, drug discovery, and patient care. We learned about real-world examples and positive feedback from healthcare professionals. We also discussed the challenges like data privacy and bias. Finally, we looked at future trends and best practices to overcome hurdles. In short, AI in Healthcare is transforming medicine with smart tools and innovative ideas. Continued research and ethical practices will keep pushing these innovations forward and make healthcare safer and more efficient for everyone.

items.title: 9 Ways to Use AI in Real Estate To Remain Competitive
items.author: Phyniks
items.content:
The real estate industry is facing an era of transformation, driven by the rapid adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI). For decades, challenges like inaccurate property valuations, time-consuming paperwork, and mismatched listings have hindered the growth of real estate businesses. Startups and industry professionals often grapple with optimizing resources and meeting evolving customer expectations.

Statistics reveal that 80% of property buyers now begin their search online, and over 63% of real estate businesses struggle to provide personalized experiences that align with buyer preferences. In a competitive market, this lack of efficiency translates to lost sales opportunities and dissatisfied clients.

This is where AI in real estate is reshaping the landscape. This blog dives into practical and impactful ways businesses can use AI to streamline operations, attract the right clients, and stay ahead in a tech-driven world.

Why Real Estate Professionals Need AI

For real estate startups, the need for efficiency and data-driven decisions is critical. Artificial intelligence in real estate offers innovative solutions to some of the most pressing issues, from managing properties to understanding market trends.

Many businesses still hesitate, unsure how to use AI in real estate effectively.

From predictive analytics that forecast market trends to dynamic virtual tours and tenant screening, AI is enabling businesses to address these pain points effectively. For startups, understanding and leveraging AI’s capabilities isn’t just optional, it’s essential to thrive in this competitive space.

Real estate startups, unlike established players, can be more agile in adopting technology, setting the stage for growth and innovation. With the right AI tools, businesses can streamline operations, reduce costs, and deliver personalized customer experiences.

How AI Is Changing Real Estate

1. Data Analysis and Market Prediction:

AI systems analyze vast amounts of data to predict market trends and property valuations. This allows businesses to make smarter, faster decisions. For instance, predictive analytics help forecast the best locations for investments, giving startups a competitive edge.

2. Enhanced Customer Interactions:

AI-powered chatbots provide 24/7 customer support, offering quick answers to queries and guiding potential buyers through the process. This improves the client experience and frees up human resources for more complex tasks.

3. Smart Property Management:

AI-driven systems help manage maintenance schedules, tenant inquiries, and energy usage. Smart tools can reduce costs while ensuring properties run efficiently — a must for startups operating on tight budgets.

9 Ways To Use AI in Real Estate Industry

Artificial intelligence is reshaping industries, and real estate is no exception. With its ability to analyze vast amounts of data, provide real-time insights, and improve operational efficiency, AI has become a crucial tool for startups and established players alike.

Let’s explore some of the transformative applications of AI and real estate.

1. AI-Powered Customer Service: Redefining Engagement Today’s buyers expect quick responses, and AI delivers. According to Statista, 83% of consumers prefer businesses offering immediate response times, which positions AI-powered tools as an essential asset.

Chatbots and virtual assistants provide instant support, answering FAQs, scheduling viewings, and suggesting tailored property recommendations around the clock. For example, Redfin’s AskRedfin chatbot responds to property-related queries in real time, reducing wait times for customers while freeing agents for higher-value tasks.

2. Smarter Property Valuation and Market Analysis Determining property value can be a complex task. AI real estate tools like Zillow’s Zestimate use machine learning to analyze location, property features, and market trends, providing accurate valuations. In 2023, Zillow reported that Zestimate’s median error rate for off-market homes was just 6.9%, making it a trusted resource for buyers and sellers.

These tools help startups and agencies gain an edge by offering precise pricing strategies that build client trust.

3. Automated Document Management for Efficiency Real estate involves extensive paperwork like leases, contracts, and more. According to PwC, businesses using AI-driven automation can cut document processing times by up to 40%.

AI automates document workflows, extracting data and identifying errors in seconds. CBRE, for example, leverages AI for lease abstraction, saving time and ensuring accuracy.

4. Hyper-Targeted Marketing with AI AI revolutionizes how businesses reach potential buyers. By analyzing user behavior and preferences, AI enables hyper-targeted marketing campaigns. Coldwell Banker, for instance, uses generative AI to create customized property descriptions that resonate with specific audiences.

Reports suggest that targeted ads driven by AI real estate solutions can improve lead conversion rates by 50%, making this an invaluable tool for startups looking to expand their client base.

5. Intelligent Property Recommendations AI simplifies property searches with smart algorithms that recommend listings based on user preferences. Platforms like Trulia employ computer vision to match customers with homes that align with their needs. This capability not only improves the customer experience but also shortens the sales cycle for businesses.

6. Predictive Analytics for Smarter Investments Investors rely on accurate predictions to make decisions. According to Deloitte, predictive analytics has helped real estate firms increase investment returns by up to 15%.

AI analyzes historical data, market trends, and economic indicators to identify emerging hotspots. By forecasting property appreciation and market demand, startups can focus on lucrative opportunities using AI real estate solutions and stay ahead of competitors.

7. Smart Home Integration and Management AI seamlessly integrates with smart home technologies, automating systems like lighting, heating, and security. This not only enhances tenant experiences but also supports energy efficiency and proactive property management for landlords.

Advanced AI sensors can adjust energy use based on occupancy patterns, reducing utility costs by up to 30%. Landlords can monitor and control properties remotely, addressing maintenance issues before they escalate, saving time and resources.

8. Dynamic Property Visualizations Generative AI creates interactive property visualizations, enabling lifelike virtual tours for buyers. This technology captures buyer interest far more effectively than traditional static images, enhancing marketing outcomes and shortening sales cycles.

Real estate startups can use AI to design multiple customization options, showing how spaces would look with different layouts or styles, giving buyers confidence before making a visit.

9. AI-Driven Tenant Screening AI real estate solution improves tenant screening by analyzing credit scores, rental histories, and employment data to provide comprehensive insights. This reduces the risk of problematic tenants and simplifies decision-making for landlords, saving time and resources.

By evaluating behavioral patterns and potential risk factors, AI-powered tools help identify reliable tenants, reducing eviction risks by 25%. Startups can also customize screening processes to comply with local rental laws while maintaining accuracy and fairness.

Overcoming Barriers in AI Adoption for Real Estate

Adopting AI real estate solutions can transform operations and enhance business outcomes. However, startups often encounter challenges in implementing AI effectively. Here are some key factors to consider for a smoother adoption process.

1. Common Barriers to AI Adoption Startups often struggle with high implementation costs, data complexity, and resistance to change. To overcome these, pilot AI tools on smaller projects and gradually scale. Lack of skilled personnel is also a common issue, which can be addressed by partnering with AI experts or training the team. A recent survey showed 42% of businesses cite a lack of skilled personnel as a major barrier to AI adoption.

2. Ethical Considerations in AI Usage AI in real estate can unintentionally introduce bias, leading to unfair decisions. To ensure fairness, startups should choose AI tools with explainable features and conduct regular audits. Human oversight is essential for accountability. A 2023 report found that 40% of real estate AI systems were linked to biased outcomes, emphasizing the need for transparency.

3. Data Complexity and Integration Challenges Real estate data can be fragmented and messy, complicating AI integration. Startups should invest in data cleaning tools and choose AI vendors offering easy integration. A Deloitte survey found that 35% of real estate professionals face difficulties in AI integration, highlighting the need for better solutions.

The Future of AI in Real Estate

AI is reshaping the real estate landscape, offering opportunities to streamline operations, enhance customer experiences, and boost business growth. Start-ups in real estate can unlock significant advantages by integrating AI thoughtfully, addressing potential barriers, and staying vigilant about ethical considerations. The key to successful AI adoption lies in understanding specific business needs, choosing the right tools, and continuously monitoring AI systems for optimal performance.

As AI and real estate continues to evolve, those who embrace it today will be better positioned to lead in tomorrow’s competitive market.

Want to take your real estate business to the next level with AI? Download our free lead magnet today!

It’s packed with practical tips and tools that will help you understand how AI can boost your operations and give you a competitive edge. Don’t miss out, get your copy now and start transforming your business!

items.title: Comparing the Leading AI Development Frameworks: TensorFlow vs PyTorch
items.author: TopDevelopers.co
items.content:
AI has become an essential component of software development, powering everything from customer service automation to predictive analytics. Businesses looking to implement AI solutions often begin by selecting the right tools to support their efforts. Among the most prominent choices are TensorFlow and PyTorch. These AI development frameworks have shaped the way machine learning models are built, trained, and deployed.

As per a 2024 report by Stack Overflow, more than 60 percent of machine learning professionals and engineers primarily use either TensorFlow or PyTorch in their workflow. This trend indicates a growing need for businesses to understand how these frameworks differ and how those differences might impact project outcomes.

This comparison is not just technical. It is about choosing a framework that aligns with business goals, technical capacity, scalability plans, and the long-term software development roadmap. Understanding their strengths and trade-offs helps decision-makers make informed choices without relying solely on developer preference or industry trends.

Comparing TensorFlow and PyTorch: A Framework-by-Framework Breakdown

Selecting the right AI development framework requires more than following popularity charts. For businesses involved in software development, each framework brings a different set of strengths, priorities, and practical outcomes. From the way models are constructed to how they are deployed, TensorFlow and PyTorch offer distinct approaches that can shape an entire AI strategy.

The comparison below explores how each framework operates, how they align with real-world needs, and what businesses can expect when using either for AI projects.

Origin and Purpose of TensorFlow and PyTorch

The foundation of any software tool lies in its origin and the intent behind its creation. Understanding why TensorFlow and PyTorch were built can help clarify how they serve different AI goals in software development.

TensorFlow was introduced by Google Brain in 2015 to support large-scale machine learning across distributed systems. Its primary objective was to offer a production-ready system that could operate efficiently on everything from cloud servers to mobile devices. TensorFlow’s early focus was on scalability, stability, and long-term support for commercial applications.

PyTorch, released by Facebook's AI Research lab in 2016, took a different path. It was built with a focus on research flexibility, experimentation, and faster prototyping. PyTorch gained traction in academic circles first and later matured into a strong production tool as the need for seamless transition from research to production grew.

Both frameworks are now supported by major tech ecosystems. TensorFlow continues to receive extensive support from Google, while PyTorch is actively maintained under the Linux Foundation with backing from Meta. These origins shape their design philosophy and offer insights into how they cater to different stages and styles of software development.

Programming Approach and Developer Experience

The core difference between TensorFlow and PyTorch lies in how each framework handles model creation and execution. This directly affects how software development teams build, test, and iterate on AI models.

TensorFlow originally used a static computation graph, which required TensorFlow developers to define the entire graph structure before running any operations. This approach made it possible to optimize performance during deployment but added complexity during model development. With the introduction of TensorFlow 2.0, the framework added eager execution by default, making the experience more dynamic and accessible, especially for those new to machine learning.

PyTorch, in contrast, has always followed a dynamic computation graph model. This means the graph is built on the fly as operations are executed, giving PyTorch developers more flexibility and immediate feedback during coding. The Pythonic design and native integration with other Python libraries also make PyTorch feel intuitive and straightforward, especially for teams familiar with Python-based software development.

Choosing between the two often depends on whether the priority is rapid experimentation or performance optimization in production. While both frameworks have improved significantly in both areas, their origins still influence how they feel to use day-to-day.

Speed and Performance in Real-World Scenarios

Performance is a key factor when comparing AI frameworks, especially for businesses aiming to scale their AI models or integrate them into large applications. TensorFlow and PyTorch offer different strengths depending on the workload and environment.

TensorFlow was built with scalability in mind and performs strongly in distributed computing environments. It includes advanced features for parallel processing, hardware acceleration, and deployment across GPUs, TPUs, and multi-node systems. These capabilities make it a strong candidate for enterprises managing large datasets or complex training cycles.

PyTorch has traditionally focused on flexibility and debugging, which made it slightly slower for certain production-level workloads in its early days. However, the introduction of TorchScript and improvements in GPU utilization have closed that gap significantly. In many benchmarks today, PyTorch performs at a comparable level to TensorFlow, with some variation depending on the use case.

For tasks that require real-time responses or low-latency inference, both frameworks offer support for model optimization and hardware integration. The decision often comes down to which tool aligns better with the existing infrastructure and whether the software development team prioritizes speed of experimentation or deployment efficiency.

Deployment Capabilities Across AI Development Platforms

Deployment is a critical phase where AI models transition from testing to real-world application. The ability to serve models efficiently across devices, platforms, and environments can influence the overall success of AI-driven software development.

TensorFlow has a well-established advantage in this area with tools like TensorFlow Serving for scalable model deployment and TensorFlow Lite for optimizing models on mobile and embedded devices. These solutions are designed to help businesses move quickly from prototyping to production while maintaining performance. The integration with cloud platforms such as Google Cloud also streamlines deployment for enterprise applications.

PyTorch has made steady progress in this space. Tools like TorchServe, developed in partnership with AWS, provide deployment support, and PyTorch Mobile allows developers to run models on mobile devices. Although these tools are newer and less mature than their TensorFlow counterparts, they have become more reliable with continuous updates and growing community support.

For businesses evaluating deployment readiness, TensorFlow currently provides a broader set of production tools. PyTorch, however, is catching up quickly and is becoming a viable choice for businesses with flexible deployment requirements or custom solutions.

Supporting Ecosystem and Integration Tools

A strong ecosystem can simplify AI implementation by providing pre-built models, visualization tools, and libraries for extended functionality. TensorFlow and PyTorch each offer ecosystems that support different workflows in software development.

TensorFlow provides an expansive set of tools, including TensorBoard for model visualization, TensorFlow Hub for reusable model components, and TensorFlow Extended (TFX) for managing the full machine learning pipeline. These tools are designed to support the entire AI lifecycle from training to deployment and monitoring. The ecosystem is particularly helpful for teams working on end-to-end machine learning pipelines.

PyTorch also has a growing ecosystem that emphasizes simplicity and interoperability. Libraries such as TorchVision, TorchText, and PyTorch Lightning offer modular functionality for vision, text, and streamlined training processes. While it may not offer the same breadth as TensorFlow, PyTorch benefits from deep integration with Python and third-party libraries like NumPy, SciPy, and Pandas.

Integration with existing tools and workflows often determines the best choice. TensorFlow provides more structured tools, while PyTorch offers greater flexibility for those building custom pipelines or experimenting with new research models.

Community Support and Industry Usage

Community support plays a significant role in how quickly teams can troubleshoot issues, adopt best practices, and find reliable learning resources. It also reflects how widely a framework is used in the industry, which can influence hiring and long-term project planning.

TensorFlow has a large global community, bolstered by extensive contributions from Google and other tech enterprises. It offers rich documentation, online tutorials, and a vast collection of shared models. TensorFlow is widely adopted across industries including finance, healthcare, and retail, which often seek stability and proven scalability in their AI solutions.

PyTorch, on the other hand, gained popularity first in academic and research communities. This support led to rapid adoption in AI labs and universities. As PyTorch matured, it also gained attention in the industry, especially among startups and product teams focused on innovation and experimentation. Many AI research papers now use PyTorch as the preferred framework, reflecting its influence in shaping next-generation AI models.

While both frameworks have strong communities, the choice can depend on the background of the software development team and the industry focus of the business. TensorFlow may appeal more to enterprises seeking comprehensive support, while PyTorch continues to attract teams that value experimentation and fast-paced iteration.

Framework Fit Based on Business Needs

Not every AI project has the same requirements, and the ideal framework should align with both technical goals and long-term business strategy. TensorFlow and PyTorch each offer advantages depending on the specific nature of the project.

TensorFlow suits businesses aiming for production-level software development with strong deployment needs. Its broad toolkit supports mobile, cloud, and embedded systems. Enterprises looking for long-term support and integration with other services may find TensorFlow a better match.

PyTorch is often favored for projects that require flexible experimentation, rapid prototyping, or academic collaboration. Startups and teams exploring new product features or custom model architectures can benefit from its streamlined interface and dynamic workflow.

Factors such as project size, time to market, team expertise, and future maintenance should all guide the decision. Instead of focusing solely on popularity, choosing a framework that aligns with actual business priorities leads to more effective and sustainable AI outcomes.

Final Thoughts on Selecting Between TensorFlow and PyTorch

Choosing between TensorFlow and PyTorch is not just a technical decision but a strategic one. For businesses investing in AI as part of their software development efforts, understanding how each framework aligns with operational goals is essential. Both AI tools are powerful, widely used, and continuously evolving, but they serve different priorities.

TensorFlow stands out with its extensive production tools and deployment capabilities. It is often the preferred choice in enterprise environments where scalability, long-term maintenance, and integration with cloud services are critical. Its structured ecosystem and robust support make it suitable for AI applications that need to move smoothly from research to real-world use.

PyTorch brings a level of flexibility that benefits fast-moving teams and innovation-driven projects. Its dynamic design supports rapid testing and changes, which is particularly useful in early-stage product development or when building custom solutions. Businesses exploring advanced AI use cases or working on emerging models often turn to PyTorch for its adaptability.

Top AI companies use a mix of both AI development frameworks based on the specific requirements of each project. This trend reflects a broader shift toward using the right tool for the task rather than following a single standard. For decision-makers, evaluating internal capabilities, deployment needs, and long-term plans helps determine which framework will offer the greatest value.

TensorFlow and PyTorch are both shaping the future of AI, and understanding their differences allows businesses to make informed, forward-looking choices.

items.title: How to connect Cursor to 100+ MCP Servers within minutes
items.author: Anmol Baranwal
items.content:
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the latest trend of 2025.

It makes it easier to scale your workflows and opens many powerful use cases.

Today, we will learn how to connect Cursor with 100+ MCP servers, with some awesome examples at the end.

In a nutshell, we are covering these topics in detail.

  1. What is Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
  2. A step-by-step guide on how to connect Cursor to 100+ fully managed MCP servers with built-in Auth.
  3. Practical use cases with examples.

I will be using Composio for MCP servers because it has built-in auth and comes with fully managed servers.

1. What is Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

Think of it as a universal connector for AI. MCP works as a plugin system for Cursor which allows you to extend the Agent’s capabilities by connecting it to various data sources and tools.

credits go to Greg Isenburg on YouTube

MCP helps you build agents and complex workflows on top of LLMs.

For example, an MCP server for Obsidian helps AI assistants search and read notes from your Obsidian vault.

→ Send emails through Gmail → Create tasks in Linear

→ Search documents in Notion

→ Post messages in Slack

→ Update records in Salesforce

All while you chat naturally with it.

Think about what this means for productivity. Tasks that used to require context switching between 5+ apps can now happen in a single conversation with your agent.

At its core, MCP follows a client-server architecture where a host application can connect to multiple servers.

There is a very interesting blog that explains architecture in deep, lifecycle, protocol, drawbacks and behind-the-scenes of MCP.

If you're looking for a perfect video and want to understand why MCP matters, check out this!

A lot of people are confused about it, so let's clarify things:

❌ - MCP can use APIs but it's a standardized interface and not a replacement for specific API functionalities.

❌ - developers can create MCP servers using straightforward protocols. There are templates, videos and more resources on the internet.

❌ - MCP servers don't directly store data but they just act as a bridge.

And it's not only restricted to remote servers. You can operate it locally as well.

2. A step-by-step guide on how to connect Cursor to 100+ fully managed MCP servers with built-in Auth.

In this section, we will be exploring how to connect Cursor with MCP servers.

If you want to explore yourself how to add and use custom MCP servers within the Cursor, read the official docs.

Install Node.js and ensure  is available in your system.

Step 2: Enable MCP server in Cursor.

You can open the command palette in Cursor with and search for cursor settings.

You will find an MCP option on the sidebar.

Step 3: Using a pre-defined MCP server.

We can also create one from scratch but let's use predefined-one for the sake of simplicity.

We will use Composio for the servers since they have built-in auth. You can find the list at mcp.composio.dev. It supports Claude and Cursor as MCP hosts.

⚡ Built-in Auth comes with support for OAuth, API keys, JWT and Basic Auth. This means you don't have to create your own login system.

⚡ Fully managed servers eliminate the need for complex setups, making it easy to integrate AI agents with tools like Gmail, Slack, Notion, Linear and more.

⚡ Better tool-calling accuracy allows AI agents to interact smoothly with integrated apps.

It also means less downtime and fewer maintenance problems. You can read more on composio.dev/mcp.

You can easily integrate with a bunch of useful MCP servers without writing any code.

With each of the options, you will find the total active users, its current version, how recently it was updated and all the available actions.

You will also get the option of Claude (MacOS), Windsurf (MacOS), TypeScript and Python for your choice of integration.

Some reasons why I decided to go with Composio:

⚡ Fully managed servers with built-in authentication (as explained earlier).

⚡ Supports over 250+ tools like Slack, Notion, Gmail, Linear, and Salesforce.

⚡ Provides 20,000+ pre-built API actions for quick integration without coding.

⚡ Can operate locally or remotely depending on your configuration needs.

⚡ It's compatible with AI agents which means it can connect AI agents to tools for tasks like sending emails, creating tasks or managing tickets in a single conversation.

If you're interested in creating from scratch. Here's how you can do it (TypeScript SDK).

You will need to install the SDK package by using this command.

npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk

This is how you can create a simple MCP server that exposes a calculator tool and some data.

Below are some of the core concepts that will help you understand the above code:

⚡ - McpServer is your core interface to the MCP protocol. It handles connection management, protocol compliance and message routing.

⚡ - This is how you expose data to LLMs. They're similar to GET endpoints in a REST API.

⚡ - Tools let LLMs take actions through your server. Unlike resources, tools are expected to perform computation and have side effects.

⚡ - Prompts are reusable templates that help LLMs easily interact with your server.

You can run your server by using (for command-line tools or direct integrations) and (for remote servers).

On the official website of MCP which is available at modelcontextprotocol.io, you will find , , and . Once you visit the repository, you will find all the necessary details in the readme.

Step 4: Integrating MCP server.

It's time to integrate one with the cursor. For now, we will be using the Hackernews MCP server.

If you don't know, Hacker News is a tech-focused news aggregator by Y Combinator, featuring user-submitted stories and discussions on startups, programming and awesome projects.

Just a couple of days ago, Cursor changed this method. I'm explaining the new and previous methods to avoid confusion.

We will need to generate the terminal command. Check this page to generate yours.

It will look something like this.

npx @composio/mcp@latest setup "https://mcp.composio.dev/hackernews/xyzxyz..." --client cursor

You can place this configuration in two locations, depending on your use case:

1) For tools specific to a project, create a  file in your project directory. This allows you to define MCP servers that are only available within that specific project.

2) For tools that you want to use across all projects, create a  file in your home directory. This makes MCP servers available in all your Cursor workspaces.

It will display the necessary actions and status green dot which indicates, that it's successfully integrated.

This is how the JSON data file looks (SSE, Python CLI, Node.js CLI in order).

We will need to generate a secure MCP URL. Check this page to generate yours.

Make sure you have type which gives you the option to insert the server URL.

It will display the necessary actions and status green dot which indicates, that it's successfully integrated.

Step 5: Using the server directly within Agent.

You can open the Chat using the command. Then you can simply ask anything related to Hackernews like find hackernews top posts of today.

newer version old version

As you can see, it will call the appropriate MCP server (if you have multiple of those) and it will accordingly use the correct action based on your prompt.

Just click accept to generate a response. Since no posts reached 100 points (a top post criterion), I tried another prompt to fetch the latest 10 posts.

Congrats! 🎉 That's how simple it really is to use MCP servers of your choice.

Hackernews was a simple case but if it's something different like GitHub where it requires a personal token, you will need to put a slightly detailed prompt like create a new repo named composio mcp and add readme with a brief info about mcp servers.

Now that we know how to integrate MCP servers into the cursor. In the next section, we are going to explore a lot of real-world examples.

3. Practical use cases with examples.

You can build lots of innovative stuff using these, so let’s explore a few that stand out. Some of these include source code (GitHub repository).

This connects Blender to Claude AI through MCP, allowing Claude to directly interact with and control Blender. This integration enables prompt assisted 3D modeling, scene creation and manipulation.

For instance, Siddharth (the owner) generated a 3D scene like low-poly dragon guarding treasure without directly working on it himself.

After adding this to the cursor MCP servers list, you will get a lot of options. However, some need to initiate a connection to avoid overloading of input response.

You can simply authenticate by copying the OAuth URL in the browser.

You will get a confirmation message once it's done.

You can also check that based on the actions of the server. As you can see, there is an active connection.

I normally wouldn't recommend using it on your official accounts because you can never be too careful. I've checked MCP servers related to DocuSign (eSignature), Cal.com (for connecting to the calendar), Notion (fetching page contents) and many more.

Most of these are simple. In the next example, we will create something much more complex.

✅ Create a web scraper using Crawl4AI.

With all these already present, you might think that it's worthless to create something new. Let's discuss a potentially complex idea.

Imagine you're a newbie and you want to improve the context for the cursor related to Python SDK docs from the official MCP repo.

In the cursor settings, you will find in the sidebar. Under that, you will find the docs section.

Then, add this link to README with an appropriate name.

Now, you can add that doc to improve context in the agent chat.

If you want to take it a step further by making the docs more accessible. There is a nice tool called Gitingest which helps you convert it to LLM readable data. You can simply replace on any GitHub URL with and it will work properly.

Now, you can use Crawl4AI which is an open source LLM Friendly Web Crawler and Scraper. It has 33k+ stars on GitHub.

Before proceeding, make sure you have installed MCP dependencies using .

You can install the crawl4ai package using the following command.

pip install -U crawl4ai

# Run post-installation setup
crawl4ai-setup

You can verify your installation using .

You can create a file and add proper code as per the docs.

You can install this server in Claude Desktop and interact with it right away by running . You can read the docs on how to integrate it.

There are more things like stealth mode and tag based content extraction which you can check in the readme.

Once you run it like crawl http://cursor.com and give me all of its sections, you will realize that it's not giving the official structure of the sections but just contents.

You can adjust it based on the docs of Crawl4AI. Now, since the docs of Python have an additional context, this will practically help directly clone the website landing pages with much better accuracy.

That's how deep the use case is for this workflow.

Here are some nice resources if you're planning to build MCP:

It's safe to say that using MCP servers in Cursor isn't that complex.

One single conversation with an Agent can help you automate complex workflows.

Let me know if you have tried it before or built something awesome.

Have a great day! Until next time :)
